items.title: EPISODE #33
items.author: Emmanuel Muturia™
items.description:
Know Your Meme 🤣

Welcomes & Hellos 🫂

New Year, New Me 🤏. While we are certainly not rebranding or anything, we hope that your 2026 has kicked off spectacularly. Are you still working on your New Year’s Resolutions? 🫣 We are aware that Life happens, and a lot of you may not have had the chance to meet us during The January Meetup, which is why you are reading this. Also, as you may have seen, we have a new Platform for you to get your monthly dose of us. More on that later, for:

This is Episode #33 of The Kotlin Kenya Newsletter 🥳…

The Open Panel Discussion 💬

Of course, most of you were still in Holiday Mode 🎄, so we opted to kick off 2026 with an Open Panel Discussion featuring Valentine Rutto, Eric Muthama, and The Chief Senior Dishwasher, GDE. Was it exciting? No, it was more than exciting. What started as a conversation on Kotlin MultiPlatform and other technical stuff almost turned into a Therapy Session where most, if not all, of our Attendees opened up on their experiences from Job Hunting to Interviewing to Salary Negotiation. If we were to list everything that was talked about, then we might as well launch The Kotlin Kenya Novel 📖. However, what stuck most with our Community Members was a simplified yet detailed Job Hunting Strategy by The Chief Senior Dishwasher, GDE. If only you saw how the room was in awe when he went through how to crush each step, so much so that everyone concluded that they were going to get Jobs by the end of 2026, unless something beyond their control prevents it. That could be you, but will you commit to it as part of your New Year's Resolutions? 😜

DroidCon x FlutterCon Kenya 2026 🗣️

Have you bought your DroidCon x FlutterCon Kenya 2026 Tickets? 🎟️ Yes, you 🫵. We are well aware that the event does not start until November, but the early bird catches the worm, right? I mean, did you not claim this to be your Year? 🫢 Let this be the one where you take charge, and you can do just that by grabbing your Tickets here: https://bit.ly/dcKE2025Tickets

New Year, New Platform 📣

As promised, we want to officially welcome you 🤗 to The Kotlin Kenya Publication. If you have been vigilant, then you must have already seen it on your way here. Yes, we heard a lot of your complaints about us having our resources all over the place 😮‍💨. Yes, we listened and realised that you too should participate in our Community more, while also increasing your Earning Potential 💰. That is why we built The Kotlin Kenya Publication, which serves as the Hub where we not only publish each Episode of The Kotlin Kenya Newsletter, but also where you can submit your Articles, have them reviewed, and see them published to a broader Audience. After all, we are a Community, and we therefore welcome all of your Contributions on this Platform as it is for you, by you. For more details on The Submission Guidelines, please check out our very first Article, written by Donald Isoe, our Chief Editor: https://medium.com/kotlinkenya-android254/submission-guidelines-kotlin-kenya-411c9ba359f2

Before You Go ✋

Hey pssst 😬. Are you interested in speaking at our upcoming Meetups? Do you have what it takes to blow the minds of our esteemed Community Members 🤯? Or, do you have that project that you cannot wait to show off to our Attendees? Well then, what are you waiting for 😳?

Click me and submit your Presentation, will ya?

Until February 👋

As this Month ends, we are excited to plan for the next one 😃. As our Community Members, we want you to gain the most Value from your Membership. What better way to do that than getting involved? How can you do that 🤓? Well, submitting your Presentations, telling a Friend to tell a Friend, and even attending the Meetups are more than enough to start you off. We look forward to seeing you in February. Until then, keep coding and do not forget to touch Grass or something 😉…

The Credits 🎬

1. Newsletter Writing, Editing, and Publishing

  • Emmanuel Muturia™

2. Speakers

  • Valentine Rutto
  • Eric Muthama
  • The Chief Senior Dishwasher, GDE

3. Organising Team

  • Emmanuel Muturia™
  • Michelle Wainaina
  • Jeremiah Gitau
  • Theophilus Kibet
  • Victor Kirui

4. Meetup Host

  • Daystar University

5. Sponsors & Partners

  • N/A…

6. Featured

  • N/A…

7. The Community Members

  • The Community Members [Android254]
  • The Community Members [Kotlin Kenya]

items.title: How To Install The pfSense Firewall on VirtualBox
items.author: Emmanuel Muturia™
items.description:
After failing a bunch of different Interviews because of this Skill Issue, I decided that enough was enough. In each scenario, all had been going well until I was asked about Firewalls. You would be forgiven for thinking that I was a Cyborg, for I always hesitated and fumbled through my words like an electrocuted Robot. Anyway, you are probably not here for the jokes, so let us get down to Business. This is: How To Install The pfSense Firewall on VirtualBox

What is pfSense?

Hold your Horses, there. Before we start installing anything, it would be best to understand what pfSense is, right? Lately, I have been made to understand that a strong enough “Why” triumphs any “How”. Anyway, enough Philosophy. pfSense is, actually, you know what? Here is the direct quote from the horse’s mouth [Emmanuel, not another Horses reference]:

pfSense® software is a free, open source customized distribution of FreeBSD specifically tailored for use as a firewall and router that is entirely managed via web interface. In addition to being a powerful, flexible firewalling and routing platform, it includes a long list of related features and a package system allowing further expandability without adding bloat and potential security vulnerabilities to the base distribution.
The pfSense project is hosted and developed by Rubicon Communications, LLC (Netgate).

~ Netgate

STEP #1 [follow me Emmanuel Muturia™]

I am not kidding. Check out my Socials everywhere [@emmanuelmuturia] for more cool stuff like this. Follow, Subscribe, you know the drill. Anyway, back to the article:

STEP #1 [THE VIRTUAL BOX]

You read that Title, did you not? Unless you intend to purchase an actual pfSense Router/Firewall, then your first step should be downloading VirtualBox. You can do this by simply navigating to their Website and retrieving the one that is based on your Operating System here: https://www.virtualbox.org/wiki/Downloads

Since you are a genius, I will not go into each since there are plenty of resources out there for you to learn how to install VirtualBox on your Operating System. You got this :)

STEP #2 [THE WALL OF FIRE]

If you were expecting a long boring tutorial then allow me to alleviate your Disappointment. Installing pfSense is relatively straightforward. First, navigate to their official Website here: https://www.pfsense.org/download/

At the time of writing this, you need to download pfSense here:

Click the “Download” button and then you will be prompted to choose an Image. Select this:

Once done, add it to the Cart and check out like you would at your preferred Online Store. You will have to create an Account for that, which should take you a few minutes. Nothing crazy. Now, before you freak out and start referencing The Economy and stuff, this is a free product, so you will not be charged at all, unless you subscribe to pfSense Plus. Relax, alright?

Anyway, once you have successfully checked out, then you should have the ISO File downloaded…

STEP #3 [THE EXTRACTION & INSTALLATION]

Your ISO File should be compressed, so the first task here is to extract it. Depending on your Operating System, you may need to use different Tools such as WinRAR for Windows and gunzip for Linux…

Once done, fire up VirtualBox and click “New”. Enter your preferred name, such as “pfSense”, and then select your extracted ISO File as the Image. Next, set the “Type” to be BSD, which should set the SubType as “FreeBSD” and Version as “FreeBSD 64-bit” by default. Allocate at least 1GB [2GB Recommended for Performance] of RAM and create at least 20GB of Hard Disk Storage Space and ensure that it is a Virtual Disk Image [VDI]…

Lastly, we need to configure the Network details so navigate to “Network” and then set Adapter #1 to be NAT [Default] and Adapter #2 to be “Internal Network”. Take note of the two MAC Addresses as they will come in handy during the installation…

Once all is done, start the Virtual Machine and then select the Default options for most of the prompts. When asked about the LAN Interface, choose the one listed below as “Active”. Also, you will be asked about your pfSense Plus Subscription, so if you have none, choose “Install CE” to install the free Community Edition…

Lastly, you will be prompted to reboot and to successfully do this, power off the Virtual Machine, navigate to “Storage” in VirtualBox, and then remove the Optical Disk Image:

Once done, start the Virtual Machine, and it should launch in Console Mode…

STEP #4 [THE CONFIGURATION]

Since this was an article on installing pfSense on VirtualBox, I will not dive into configuring it since I have linked a Video that does it better. It will also serve as a reference for the preceding steps, so be sure to watch it from start to end and ensure that you have set up everything correctly…

CONCLUSION

Well, then, here we are. Congratulate yourself for taking the first step in mastering Firewalls. After all, the journey of a thousand miles begins with one step, eh? Stay tuned for Content around Firewalls, and warn your Interviewers since we will be crushing those Firewall questions from here moving forward, or am I right?

Anyway, I hope that you found value in this article and with all that said and done, see you soon…

REFERENCES

ZacsTech. (2025, April 4). How to Install pfSense on VirtualBox (2025). YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Y-Dj8lHmXy8

items.title: How To Fix The “{“message”:”Internal Server Error”}” Error in AWS
items.author: Emmanuel Muturia™
items.description:
You are well on your way to provisioning your AWS Infrastructure. Perhaps like me, you are setting up the Backend of that soon-to-be-viral Mobile App of yours, or maybe you are a Master Backend Engineer who spins Backends to life by whistling some commands to your workstation. Regardless of your use case, chances are you ran into an error message that left you wondering what part of your humongous Infrastructure was misbehaving. I mean, let us face it. Unless you are a Solutions Architect or similar, chances are you have not mastered the tip of the iceberg when it comes to the services offered by AWS. What error message am I referring to, though? Ladies and gentlemen, I present to you:

{"message":"Internal Server Error"}

THE BACKGROUND

I was working on a project [to be shared soon with you guys], and part of it consisted of deploying a REST API [AWS API Gateway] using AWS Lambda. At first, everything was working smoothly, and I had encountered no errors. “Something is not right…”, I thought. Of course, any regular individual will wonder what was up with my pessimism. However, I know that you, a Techie, understand why I was not confident in the smooth flow of events. True to my suspicions, that error message slapped me just as I was about to deploy my Infrastructure, or at least a portion of it. Talk about timing, right? Anyway, I kept receiving that error and due to its ambiguity, I had trouble working out why it was re-occurring despite my Infrastructure’s configuration looking like it was rightly set up. That was where my troubleshooting session began, and what I found made me laugh as if it had made fun of me, which I guess it did. Now, let me take you through how I diagnosed the issue and what it led me to, so that you can hopefully experience the same…

THE DIAGNOSIS

STEP 1] THE INFRASTRUCTURE CONFIGURATION

Of course, my first step was to double-check my Infrastructure’s configuration. As I was using Terraform to provision my Infrastructure, I checked my .tf files for any misconfigurations. In your case, this could mean double-checking your configuration on the AWS Console or whatever resources you are currently using to set up your AWS Infrastructure. Trust me, most errors of this nature originate from here…

STEP 2] THE LOGS

As expected, my next step was to check my Logs, specifically CloudWatch Logs. The easiest way to do this is by navigating to CloudWatch > Log Groups. From here, I selected the Log Group that was tied to my Infrastructure. For you, this shouldshed more light on what the issue could be…

Spoiler Alert: This led me to the solution…

STEP 3] THE LAMBDA TEST

At this point, the CloudWatch Logs were not helpful as I had yet to update them to match my real-time REST API Requests. My next best option was to navigate to AWS Lambda and test the Lambda Function manually to see what the issue is. Oh, in case I did not mention, I was essentially using a Lambda Function to make a REST API Call to my AWS Infrastructure. Again, your use case could be different, but this is usually the most common one that results in the error message that this article focuses on. Now, what happened here?

Well, I tested the Lambda Function, and as expected, it failed massively. Alright, maybe that is an overstatement, but it did fail. The reason was rather silly, and I do not want you to click off, so let me spare you the details. It was a file path referencing issue. Can you believe that? I know. How surprising. This was not the end of the story, though…

I tested the Lambda Function again, and it still threw the error. This time, the Logs were different. I can already sense your excitement, and yes, I too experienced it. This is what I got:

[More Logs here...]
"errorType": "AuthorizationErrorException"
[More Logs here too...]

You already know where this is headed, so let me cut right to the chase. It turned out that I did not assign my Lambda Function the right permissions to make that REST API Call. This was the culprit all along…

[This is where I laughed at myself, by the way…]

WHAT SHOULD YOU TAKE AWAY FROM THIS?

  • Please read on, understand, and practise IAM and IAM Policies in AWS…
  • When provisioning your AWS Infrastructure, always double-check if each component has been assigned the right permissions for its task. If you are in Cyber Security, you might recognise a similar concept known as The Principle of Least Privilege…
  • Again, please read more on IAM in AWS. I do not know why it had to be made this difficult to understand, at least in the beginning, alright? Just read and understand it, and you should be good to go. I hope…

CONCLUSION

By this point, you now understand the common pitfalls that contribute to the dreaded [well, not so much] “Server Error” error when setting up an AWS Infrastructure. If this has not solved your error, then read it again. If it still does not fix your error, then just quit and use another Cloud provider. There are plenty of fish in the sea. I am kidding, though. If your error persists, then I highly recommend that you revisit your AWS Infrastructure and revise your components before testing them on the console while monitoring the logs. Alright, it is time for me to go back to building, and I hope to share with you what I am working on…

What error made you laugh at yourself like I did before publishing this article?

items.title: The Android Architecture [feat. The System Apps]
items.author: Emmanuel Muturia™
items.description:
You have made it, dear reader. You may applaud yourself now. After traversing the technicalities of The Android Architecture, it is now time to talk about the good stuff. Well, the ones that kinda involve us who love to build Android apps and stuff. This article concludes this series by talking about The System Apps. As they say, “Save the best for last…”. I am not sure if it is “they” who said it but I believe it anyway…

1. Overview: What Are System Apps?

System apps are the pre-installed applications that come with the Android operating system. They form the core suite of software essential for device functionality, ranging from the phone dialer, contacts, and messaging to core services like settings, system UI, and more. Developed by the OEMs or the Android Open Source Project (AOSP), these apps run with elevated privileges, have direct integration with the OS, and provide a consistent, secure, and optimized user experience. They are critical for booting the device, managing hardware interactions, and offering fundamental services that third-party apps cannot replicate…

Analogy
Think of system apps as the essential features built into a car by the manufacturer — like the dashboard instruments, the steering system, braking controls, and integrated safety features. These components are not optional add-ons; they are built into the car’s design to ensure safety, reliability, and a seamless driving experience, much like system apps are integral to the Android experience…

2. Core Functions and Essential Services

System apps provide the backbone of daily operations on an Android device. They include core functionalities such as telephony services, contact management, messaging, camera functionality, and system settings. Beyond these, they often include system-level utilities like file managers, network configuration tools, and update managers. Their design is tightly coupled with system APIs, ensuring high reliability and optimal performance even under demanding conditions…

Analogy
Imagine a car equipped with all the built-in systems necessary for daily operation: the Engine Control Unit [ECU] that manages engine performance, integrated infotainment for navigation and communication, and safety systems like airbags and ABS. Just as these built-in features ensure the car functions seamlessly from the moment you start it, system apps ensure that your Android device is fully operational and optimized for everyday tasks…

3. Privileges, Security, and Trust

System apps operate with a higher level of privilege compared to third-party applications. They can access secure system resources, interact with hardware at a low level, and execute critical system commands. This elevated trust level is managed through stringent security policies and digital signatures. The framework ensures that these apps are vetted, maintained, and updated by trusted sources to protect against vulnerabilities and unauthorised access, thereby safeguarding the overall system integrity…

Analogy
Consider these apps as the factory-installed security features in a car — such as the anti-theft system, advanced braking controls, and stability management systems. Just as these components are rigorously tested and certified to protect the driver, system apps run with heightened privileges and robust security measures to protect the device and ensure reliable operation…

4. Integration with the Operating System and Hardware

System apps are deeply integrated with the Android operating system. They work in tandem with core system services, the Hardware Abstraction Layer [HAL], and other native components to deliver seamless functionality. This integration allows them to leverage low-level hardware capabilities, respond to system-wide events, and interact directly with other system components. Their close coupling with the OS facilitates rapid updates and tight coordination during system boot, runtime configuration, and even shutdown procedures…

Analogy
Think of system apps as the car’s integrated control systems that work hand-in-hand with the vehicle’s mechanics. For instance, the car’s ABS works closely with sensors, the braking system, and the ECU to provide optimal stopping power. Similarly, system apps integrate with Android’s core services and hardware interfaces to deliver a smooth, coordinated performance throughout the device’s lifecycle…

5. Performance, Resource Management, and Optimization

System apps are optimised for performance and efficient resource management. They are built to handle high loads and execute critical functions with minimal latency. Developers use extensive profiling, real-time debugging, and hardware-specific optimizations to ensure these apps consume fewer resources while delivering robust performance. Their code is often tightly optimised to balance power consumption, responsiveness, and system stability, which is crucial in resource-constrained mobile environments…

Analogy
Imagine a finely tuned car where every component is optimised for maximum performance and fuel efficiency — from the engine’s high-compression design to aerodynamic bodywork. The car’s systems are calibrated to reduce friction, minimize energy loss, and deliver peak performance under various driving conditions. In the same way, system apps are engineered to run swiftly, manage resources effectively, and keep the device operating at its best…

6. Update Mechanisms and System Integrity

System apps are subject to strict update protocols to ensure that the device remains secure, stable, and feature-rich. Updates are typically delivered via Over-The-Air [OTA] system updates or through pre-approved channels from the device manufacturer. These updates often include security patches, performance enhancements, and new features, and they undergo rigorous testing to maintain system integrity. The centralised management of these apps allows for consistent updates across the entire device ecosystem, ensuring that all critical services are current and secure…

Analogy
Think of this process as the periodic service and software updates that modern cars receive — updates to the onboard computer, firmware upgrades for safety systems, and recalls for critical components. Just as these updates keep the car safe, reliable, and up-to-date with the latest technology, system app updates ensure that your Android device remains secure and efficient, maintaining a high standard of performance over its lifetime…

7. Evolution, Customisation, and Future Trends

The landscape of system apps is continually evolving as Android grows and adapts to new technological trends. Innovations such as machine learning, augmented reality, and advanced connectivity are increasingly being integrated into system-level functionalities. Future system apps are expected to incorporate more adaptive user interfaces, context-aware services, and predictive features that enhance usability and efficiency. Customisation is also becoming more prominent, allowing OEMs to tailor system apps to differentiate their devices while still adhering to core Android standards. This dynamic evolution ensures that system apps remain at the forefront of mobile technology, driving innovation and enhancing the overall user experience…

Analogy
Consider the evolution of automotive technology — from classic mechanical instruments to digital dashboards, autonomous driving features, and smart connectivity. As cars integrate more advanced systems, they become not only safer and more efficient but also increasingly personalized to the driver’s preferences. Likewise, the evolution of system apps promises a future where your device is more intelligent, adaptable, and seamlessly integrated with emerging technologies, delivering an ever-improving driving [User] experience.

CONCLUSION

Apart from the desire to drive your Car right now, I hope that you have now gotten a better and more technical understanding of The System Apps in the context [pun intended] of The Android Architecture. While this may be the end of this series whose name I still do not know, please stay tuned for more content so that we can learn together, right? Until then, I hope that you remember what you learned today each time you work on your car[s]…

REFERENCES

Platform Architecture. (2023, May 4). Android Developers. https://developer.android.com/guide/platform

items.title: The Android Architecture [feat. The J*va API Framework]
items.author: Emmanuel Muturia™
items.description:
Well done, dear reader. You are more than halfway there. Before you applaud yourself, though, I need to warn you about this article. While I value the impact of the layer that will be discussed today, you need to be wary of the technology behind it. Rumour has it that it was almost rendered obsolete in the world of Android once its successor took over since the year 2017. Yes. That is what we are dealing with today. Anyway, this article talks about the different APIs that are responsible for the rich features of Android and how they work together to bring Android apps [both Developer Apps and System Apps] to life. Today, we will be talking about The J*va API Framework…

1. Overview: What Is the J*va API Framework?

The J*va API Framework is the cornerstone of Android application development. It is a vast collection of classes, interfaces, and libraries built primarily on J*va (and increasingly Kotlin) that developers use to build applications. This framework provides everything from UI components and system services to networking, data management, and more. It abstracts complex underlying system operations and offers a standardized set of tools that ensure apps can interact consistently with the Android operating system across different devices…

Analogy
Think of the J*va API Framework as the car’s dashboard and control center. Just as a driver uses the dashboard to monitor speed, fuel, and engine status without needing to understand the intricacies of the engine, developers use the API to control and interact with the device’s functionalities without diving into low-level system details…

2. Core Components: Standard Libraries and Essential Services

At its heart, the framework comprises a collection of core libraries and packages — such as java.lang, java.util, and Android-specific packages like android.app, android.content, android.view, and more. These libraries provide fundamental services including event handling, resource management, layout rendering, and connectivity. They serve as modular building blocks that facilitate the rapid development of robust applications by providing pre-built, well-tested functionality…

Analogy
Imagine these core libraries as the standard components found in every modern car — like the engine, transmission, braking system, and infotainment controls. These parts are designed to work seamlessly together, giving you a reliable and familiar experience regardless of the car’s brand, much like how the Java API Framework provides a consistent set of tools for app development…

3. Application Components and Architecture

The framework defines the structure and lifecycle of key Android application components such as Activities, Services, BroadcastReceivers, and Content Providers. These components dictate how apps manage user interactions, background processes, and data exchange. The framework enforces architectural patterns [e.g., MVC, MVP, MVVM] to ensure that apps are scalable, maintainable, and responsive. It also governs the flow of information and control among these components to provide a coherent and integrated user experience…

Analogy
Consider these application components as the different subsystems of a car. An Activity is like the car’s main driving mode, Services resemble the background systems such as climate control or navigation, BroadcastReceivers act like the car’s warning lights that alert you to changes, and Content Providers are akin to the car’s storage compartments that organize your gear. Together, they form a well-orchestrated system that delivers a smooth and reliable driving experience…

4. Abstraction and Ease-of-Use: Simplifying Complexity

One of the primary strengths of the Java API Framework is its ability to abstract complex operations into simple, high-level interfaces. This abstraction hides the intricate details of hardware management, network protocols, and system security from developers. Instead of writing low-level code, developers can invoke straightforward methods to perform complex tasks such as drawing graphics, handling user input, or accessing remote data. This approach not only accelerates development but also reduces the likelihood of errors…

Analogy
Think of this abstraction as the car’s steering wheel and pedals. You don’t need to understand the combustion process or the intricacies of the suspension system to steer, accelerate, or brake. The intuitive controls simplify the driving process, just as the J*va API Framework simplifies app development by hiding complex system internals behind user-friendly interfaces…

5. Consistency and Integration: A Unified Development Experience

The Java API Framework ensures that all Android applications have a consistent behavior across diverse hardware and software configurations. It integrates seamlessly with other critical layers of the Android ecosystem, such as the Android Runtime (ART), the Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL), and native libraries. This integration allows developers to build applications that can leverage system resources efficiently, adhere to best practices for security and performance, and provide a uniform user experience regardless of the underlying device architecture…

Analogy
Imagine driving on a highway where every vehicle, regardless of its make or model, follows the same road rules and uses standardized road signs. The consistent framework acts like this standardized system, ensuring that every “driver” (app) interacts predictably with the “road” (device), leading to a smooth and harmonious journey for everyone…

6. Development Tools and Ecosystem Support

The Java API Framework is supported by a robust ecosystem of development tools and environments — most notably Android Studio. These tools offer features like code autocompletion, real-time debugging, profiling, and extensive documentation that streamline the development process. They empower developers to design, test, and refine their applications with efficiency and precision. Additionally, comprehensive API documentation and community resources help troubleshoot issues and encourage best practices across the developer community…

Analogy
Think of these development tools as the car’s Onboard Diagnostics System [OBD] and navigation aids. Just as these systems help you monitor your car’s health, troubleshoot issues, and plan the best routes, the ecosystem surrounding the Java API Framework guides developers through building high-quality applications by providing real-time feedback, suggestions, and a wealth of information to keep the “vehicle” of your app in top shape…

7. Evolution and Future Trends: Driving Innovation Forward

The J*va API Framework is continuously evolving to meet the demands of modern application development. It adapts to new programming paradigms such as reactive programming and functional interfaces, and it integrates smoothly with newer languages like Kotlin. Ongoing improvements in performance, security, and developer ergonomics ensure that the framework remains at the cutting edge of mobile technology. This evolution enables developers to build innovative applications that leverage the latest hardware advancements and user experience trends…

Analogy
Consider the evolution of automotive technology — from basic, manual cars to sophisticated vehicles equipped with self-driving features and advanced safety systems. The Java API Framework is on a similar trajectory, continually upgrading its features and capabilities. As newer “models” of the framework emerge, developers can expect enhanced performance, smarter interfaces, and a more seamless, intuitive driving experience — ensuring that their apps stay ahead of the technological curve…

UP NEXT

The System Apps: https://medium.com/@emmanuelmuturia/97413b19dca1

CONCLUSION

Apart from the desire to drive your Car right now, I hope that you have now gotten a better and more technical understanding of The J*va API Framework in the context [pun intended] of The Android Architecture. Stay tuned for both a higher-level summary of this topic as well as the next layer of The Android Architecture. Until then, I hope that you remember what you learned today each time you go for your road trip :)…

REFERENCES

Platform Architecture. (2023, May 4). Android Developers. https://developer.android.com/guide/platform

items.title: The Android Architecture [feat. Native C/C++ Libraries & Android Runtime]
items.author: Emmanuel Muturia™
items.description:
They say that “Third time’s a charm”. While I am still not sure who “they” refers to, I agree with them because today’s article is a special one as it talks about not 1 but 2 components of The Android Architecture. Previously, we learnt that The Hardware Abstraction Layer [HAL]provides the rest of Android access to The Linux Kernel, which is the core of the OS. Now, have you ever wondered what the building blocks of these components are? Also, have you asked yourself how all those Android apps can run on a single device without instantly depleting its battery level due to their unique performance requirements. If that is you, then this is your article for it talks about The NativeC/C++ Libraries and The Android Runtime. Again, the Car analogy will be used for illustration purposes…

1. Overview: What Are They?

The Native C/C++ Libraries are a set of core, low-level libraries written in C and C++ that power performance-critical tasks — such as graphics rendering, media processing, and hardware access — within Android…

In contrast, the Android Runtime [ART] is the managed runtime environment that executes Android applications. ART replaces Dalvik with Ahead-Of-Time [AOT] and Just-In-Time [JIT] compilation techniques, advanced garbage collection, and improved memory management. Together, these components form the backbone of Android’s performance: the native libraries provide near–hardware-level speed and efficiency, while ART manages application execution with smart optimizations…

Analogy
Think of the Native C/C++ Libraries as the car’s finely engineered engine and essential mechanical components that deliver raw horsepower. ART, on the other hand, is like the sophisticated onboard computer system that monitors, optimizes, and adapts engine performance in real time. Together, they ensure your vehicle not only has the power to accelerate but also runs smoothly and efficiently…

2. Performance and Efficiency: Raw Power and Optimised Control

Native libraries are compiled to machine code, allowing them to run with minimal overhead and at near-hardware speeds. This makes them ideal for tasks that demand high performance, such as 3D graphics or real-time media processing. ART enhances this performance by compiling apps Ahead-Of-Time [AOT] for fast startup and using Just-In-Time [JIT] compilation to optimise frequently executed code paths during runtime. Additionally, ART’s efficient garbage collection ensures that memory is reclaimed promptly, keeping the system responsive even under heavy workloads…

Analogy
Imagine the native libraries as a high-performance engine built for raw power, capable of delivering maximum horsepower. ART is the finely tuned transmission and Engine Control Unit [ECU] that adjusts gear ratios on the fly, ensuring that the raw power is harnessed smoothly and efficiently. Together, they provide both the muscle and the smart control that let your car accelerate quickly and maintain peak performance under various driving conditions…

3. Standardised APIs and Integration: Consistency Across the Board

The Android Native Development Kit [NDK] provides a consistent set of APIs that allow developers to harness the power of native libraries, ensuring that performance-critical code works uniformly across different hardware. ART leverages the J*va Native Interface [JNI] to bridge the gap between managed Java/Kotlin code and native C/C++ code, enabling seamless interoperability. This standardised approach minimizes fragmentation, ensures compatibility, and allows both layers to work in concert without the developer needing to manage low-level hardware specifics…

Analogy
Picture a car assembly line where every vehicle is built with standardised components — engines, brakes, and electronics — that ensure consistent performance across models. The integration via JNI is like a finely engineered gear shift that smoothly transitions power from the engine to the drivetrain, ensuring the car operates reliably regardless of individual parts. In this way, both the native libraries and ART work together under a unified standard, much like standardized car parts ensure a dependable driving experience…

4. Graphics, Media, and Real-Time Operations: Handling the High-Octane Tasks

Native libraries, including OpenGL ES, Vulkan, and media frameworks like libstagefright, are optimized for intensive graphics rendering and media processing tasks. They provide the raw computational power necessary for high-quality visuals and real-time audio/video playback. ART complements these capabilities by ensuring that the application code, which may invoke these libraries frequently, runs with minimal overhead through dynamic optimizations. This cooperation results in a smooth, responsive user experience even during resource-intensive operations…

Analogy
Think of the native libraries as a turbocharged engine that delivers instantaneous power for rapid acceleration and dynamic performance — ideal for high-speed racing. ART acts like an adaptive transmission that seamlessly shifts gears based on driving conditions, ensuring that every burst of power is effectively translated into smooth motion. Combined, they create a driving experience where raw performance and refined control work together to handle even the most demanding scenarios…

5. Memory Management and Garbage Collection: Sustaining the Drive

Native C/C++ code is designed for direct and efficient memory management, often requiring the developer to handle allocation and deallocation with care. ART introduces an advanced garbage collection mechanism that automates memory reclamation, reducing the risk of leaks and latency issues. This synergy ensures that while native libraries provide the power through efficient, low-level memory operations, ART keeps the system’s memory clean and well-organized, maintaining high performance and stability over prolonged use…

Analogy
Imagine the native memory management as the car’s precise fuel injection system that delivers just the right amount of fuel to each engine component, ensuring optimal performance. ART’s garbage collection is akin to an automated maintenance system that continuously cleans and adjusts the engine, preventing clogs and wear. Together, they keep your car running at peak efficiency — always ready for the next drive without unexpected breakdowns…

6. Debugging, Profiling, and Optimisation: Tuning the Machine

A robust ecosystem of tools — such as systrace, gdb, and performance profilers — is available to diagnose and optimize both native libraries and ART’s execution. Developers can profile low-level C/C++ code for bottlenecks, memory leaks, or inefficient loops, while ART’s diagnostic tools provide insights into runtime behavior, garbage collection events, and JIT performance. This comprehensive approach to debugging and optimization ensures that applications are not only high-performing but also reliable and resource-efficient under diverse conditions…

Analogy
Think of it as having a state-of-the-art onboard diagnostic system in your sports car that continuously monitors engine performance, fuel efficiency, and mechanical integrity. Advanced diagnostic tools help mechanics fine-tune the engine and transmission, ensuring peak performance. Similarly, the debugging and profiling tools for native libraries and ART work together to “tune” your Android device, ensuring that every component operates in harmony for the ultimate driving experience…

7. Evolution and Future Trends: Driving Innovation Forward

Both the Native C/C++ Libraries and ART are evolving rapidly to meet the demands of next-generation Android applications. Modern C/C++ standards (like C++11 and beyond) and improved compiler optimisations continue to enhance native performance, while ART is integrating predictive optimizations, machine learning techniques, and more efficient memory management strategies. These advancements pave the way for new hardware innovations, better energy efficiency, and more secure and responsive applications — ensuring that Android remains at the cutting edge of mobile technology…

Just as modern cars are continually upgraded with innovations to enhance safety, performance, and efficiency, the native libraries and ART are being reengineered to deliver a futuristic driving experience. Together, they ensure that your Android “vehicle” stays ahead of the curve, ready to tackle tomorrow’s challenges with state-of-the-art technology…

They say that “Third time’s a charm”. While I am still not sure who “they” refers to, I agree with them because today’s article is a special one as it talks about not 1 but 2 components of The Android Architecture. Previously, we learnt that The Hardware Abstraction Layer [HAL]provides the rest of Android access to The Linux Kernel, which is the core of the OS. Now, have you ever wondered what the building blocks of these components are? Also, have you asked yourself how all those Android apps can run on a single device without instantly depleting its battery level due to their unique performance requirements. If that is you, then this is your article for it talks about The NativeC/C++ Libraries and The Android Runtime. Again, the Car analogy will be used for illustration purposes…

1. Overview: What Are They?

The Native C/C++ Libraries are a set of core, low-level libraries written in C and C++ that power performance-critical tasks — such as graphics rendering, media processing, and hardware access — within Android…

In contrast, the Android Runtime [ART] is the managed runtime environment that executes Android applications. ART replaces Dalvik with Ahead-Of-Time [AOT] and Just-In-Time [JIT] compilation techniques, advanced garbage collection, and improved memory management. Together, these components form the backbone of Android’s performance: the native libraries provide near–hardware-level speed and efficiency, while ART manages application execution with smart optimizations…

Analogy
Think of the Native C/C++ Libraries as the car’s finely engineered engine and essential mechanical components that deliver raw horsepower. ART, on the other hand, is like the sophisticated onboard computer system that monitors, optimizes, and adapts engine performance in real time. Together, they ensure your vehicle not only has the power to accelerate but also runs smoothly and efficiently…

Native libraries are compiled to machine code, allowing them to run with minimal overhead and at near-hardware speeds. This makes them ideal for tasks that demand high performance, such as 3D graphics or real-time media processing. ART enhances this performance by compiling apps Ahead-Of-Time [AOT] for fast startup and using Just-In-Time [JIT] compilation to optimise frequently executed code paths during runtime. Additionally, ART’s efficient garbage collection ensures that memory is reclaimed promptly, keeping the system responsive even under heavy workloads…

ART complements these capabilities by ensuring that the application code, which may invoke these libraries frequently, runs with minimal overhead through dynamic optimizations. This cooperation results in a smooth, responsive user experience even during resource-intensive operations…

ART acts like an adaptive transmission that seamlessly shifts gears based on driving conditions, ensuring that every burst of power is effectively translated into smooth motion. Combined, they create a driving experience where raw performance and refined control work together to handle even the most demanding scenarios…

ART introduces an advanced garbage collection mechanism that automates memory reclamation, reducing the risk of leaks and latency issues. This synergy ensures that while native libraries provide the power through efficient, low-level memory operations, ART keeps the system’s memory clean and well-organized, maintaining high performance and stability over prolonged use…

items.title: The Android Architecture [feat. The Hardware Abstraction Layer]
items.author: Emmanuel Muturia™
items.description:
Previously on whatever this series is called, we talked about The Linux Kernel and everything about it concerning The Android Architecture. Today, we will be moving a notch higher, literally. Of course, The Linux Kernel would be useless to Android if it was not accessible. Since accessing it directly would be quite complex, there is a layer whose sole purpose is to make this accessibility a little bit easier. Today, you will be learning more about The Hardware Abstraction Layer [HAL] and yes, we will be using a Car analogy like we did last time so buckle up…

1. What is the Hardware Abstraction Layer [HAL]?

The Hardware Abstraction Layer [HAL] is a vital layer in Android’s architecture that serves as the intermediary between the Android Framework and the device’s low-level hardware drivers. Written mostly in C/C++, it defines a set of standard APIs that encapsulate the specifics of hardware operations. By providing a uniform interface, the HAL allows higher-level Android services to interact with a wide range of hardware implementations without needing to understand each device’s unique details. This modular structure not only speeds up development but also ensures compatibility across diverse hardware configurations…

Analogy
Think of the HAL as the car’s central control module that abstracts the engine’s intricate workings. Just as a driver doesn’t need to know how fuel injection, ignition timing, or turbo boosting work to drive the car, Android developers can rely on the HAL to handle the low-level hardware interactions. It enables you to focus on the “driving” [building your app] without needing to understand every mechanical detail…

2. Standardised Interface: The Universal Dashboard

The HAL establishes standardised interfaces that hardware vendors must implement, which means that regardless of the manufacturer or specific hardware variations, the Android Framework communicates through a consistent set of function calls. These interfaces are often defined in hardware-specific libraries and are critical for ensuring that disparate hardware components behave predictably. This standardisation minimises fragmentation and simplifies the integration of new hardware into the Android ecosystem…

Analogy
Imagine every car having a universal dashboard. No matter the make or model, the controls — be it for climate, navigation, or entertainment — are laid out in a familiar pattern. Similarly, HAL provides a “dashboard” of APIs that ensure uniform control over hardware components, so that the Android system can operate every device with the same set of commands…

3. Modularity: Plug-and-Play Components

The design of the HAL is modular, meaning that each hardware component (like the camera, GPS, or Bluetooth) has its own dedicated module. These modules can be independently developed, updated, or replaced without affecting the rest of the system. This plug-and-play nature not only supports hardware diversity but also facilitates faster innovation and easier troubleshooting. Vendors can supply custom modules tailored to their hardware’s capabilities while still adhering to the standard HAL interface…

Analogy
Think of it as a car built with modular components: you can swap out the stereo, upgrade the air conditioning, or replace the navigation system independently. Each module works as a self-contained unit that plugs into the car’s overall system, ensuring that even if one component is upgraded or replaced, the rest of the vehicle continues to operate seamlessly…

4. Bridging Framework and Drivers: The Wiring Harness

The HAL acts as the critical bridge between the Android Framework and the underlying hardware drivers. When an application requests a hardware service, the request passes through the Android Framework, reaches the HAL, and is then translated into low-level commands that the hardware drivers understand. This process involves Inter-Process Communication (IPC) mechanisms and well-defined protocols that ensure commands are executed correctly and efficiently. The HAL essentially decouples the high-level application logic from the low-level hardware operations…

Analogy
Imagine the car’s wiring harness — a network of wires and control modules that links the dashboard controls to the engine, brakes, and other subsystems. When you press the accelerator or apply the brakes, signals travel through this harness to enact the desired response. Similarly, the HAL translates and routes software commands from the Android Framework to the correct hardware driver, ensuring every “signal” reaches its destination accurately...

5. Vendor Customisation: Tailored Features Under the Hood

While the HAL sets the standard for hardware interaction, it also allows vendors some flexibility to implement device-specific features. Manufacturers can create custom HAL modules that expose additional capabilities unique to their hardware. This means that while basic functionality remains consistent across devices, specialised features — like advanced camera capabilities or proprietary sensor functions — can be integrated without disrupting the overall system architecture. This balance between standardisation and customisation is key to Android’s versatility…

Analogy
Think of this as car manufacturers offering branded enhancements — like a premium sound system, sport-tuned suspension, or an advanced infotainment package. Even though every car adheres to basic controls and safety features, these extra touches allow each manufacturer to offer a unique driving experience. In the same way, vendor-specific HAL modules add tailored functionality while still interfacing with Android’s standardised framework…

6. Performance and Responsiveness: Optimising The Drive

An efficiently implemented HAL is critical for achieving high performance and responsiveness in an Android device. By minimizing the overhead in communication between the Android Framework and hardware drivers, the HAL contributes directly to faster execution of hardware commands and better overall system performance. This efficiency is particularly important for tasks that demand real-time processing, such as multimedia rendering or sensor data processing, where delays can negatively impact user experience.

Analogy
Consider the role of an optimised Engine Control Unit (ECU) in a high-performance car. When the ECU efficiently processes sensor data and adjusts engine parameters on the fly, the car delivers smooth acceleration and responsiveness. Likewise, an optimized HAL ensures that hardware commands are processed swiftly, translating into a more responsive and efficient device — much like the seamless performance of a finely tuned car…

7. Evolution and Future of HAL: Upgrading Your Ride

As new hardware technologies and capabilities emerge, the HAL must evolve to support these advancements. This ongoing development is essential for integrating next-generation components, such as augmented reality sensors or AI accelerators, into the Android ecosystem. Developers and hardware vendors work together to update HAL specifications and interfaces, ensuring backward compatibility while enabling innovation. This evolution not only supports improved functionality and performance but also helps maintain a cohesive ecosystem despite rapid technological changes.

Analogy
This is similar to the evolution of cars — from basic combustion engines to sophisticated hybrids and electric vehicles equipped with autonomous driving features. Just as cars receive periodic upgrades and new technologies to enhance performance and safety, the HAL is continuously refined to incorporate cutting-edge hardware advancements, ensuring that your “vehicle” [Android device] remains state-of-the-art…

UP NEXT

The Native C/C++ Libraries and Android Runtime: https://medium.com/@emmanuelmuturia/8f9e05ede41b

CONCLUSION

Apart from the desire to drive your Car right now, I hope that you have now gotten a better and more technical understanding of The Hardware Abstraction Layer [HAL] in the context [pun intended] of The Android Architecture. Stay tuned for both a higher-level summary of this topic as well as the next layer of The Android Architecture. Until then, I hope that you remember what you learned today each time you go for your road trip :)…

REFERENCES

Platform Architecture. (2023, May 4). Android Developers. https://developer.android.com/guide/platform

items.title: The Android Architecture [feat. The Linux Kernel]
items.author: Emmanuel Muturia™
items.description:
If you are like me, you have probably fallen into the trap of trying to master Android stuff without going into the nooks and crannies. This, of course, results in a stammering session during interviews that you probably have PTSD about. Then again, they said that “The Devil is in the detail”. I am not sure who “they” is or how that expression is relevant here but I guess I needed to sound intelligent if I was going to take you through a deeper dive into one of the most popular topics in Android: The Android Architecture…

THE BACKGROUND

While a lot has been happening ever since the creation of Android, the fundamentals remain fundamental to our understanding of not just the OS itself but also the apps that we love to build for it. This article is a fun and technical explainer of the different building blocks that comprise Android. Today, we will be focusing on the deepest layer of The Android Architecture: The Linux Kernel…

1. Overview: What is The Linux Kernel?

The Linux Kernel is the central component of Android. It acts as the core bridge between hardware and software, managing resources, scheduling tasks, and enforcing security policies. Without it, Android apps would not be able to communicate with the underlying hardware, making it the essential foundation of Android’s functionality…

Analogy
Think of the Linux Kernel as the engine of your car. Just as the engine converts fuel into motion and powers every function of the vehicle, the kernel powers all operations on your Android device. It is the driving force that ensures every component works in harmony, turning user commands into real-world actions…

2. Process Management: Orchestrating the System’s Pulse

Process Management in the Linux Kernel is responsible for scheduling, executing, and managing all active processes. It determines the order and duration for which each process uses the CPU, ensuring that system resources are efficiently shared among all running applications…

Analogy
Imagine that Process Management is your car’s transmission system. Just as a transmission shifts gears to optimise the engine’s power output based on driving conditions, the kernel’s scheduler dynamically allocates CPU time to various processes. This careful management prevents “traffic jams” in processing and ensures smooth, responsive performance — much like a well-tuned car seamlessly shifting through gears during a drive…

3. Memory Management: Allocating and Optimising Resources

Memory Management in the kernel involves dynamically allocating, tracking, and reclaiming Memory for processes. Techniques like Virtual Memory, Paging, and Caching ensure that each application has the necessary space to operate without interfering with others, maintaining system stability and efficiency…

Analogy
Consider that Memory Management is the car’s fuel injection and storage system. Just as fuel injectors precisely deliver the right amount of fuel to each engine cylinder and the car’s compartments are organised to store essentials, the kernel allocates the appropriate amount of memory to each process. This ensures that every part of the “engine” runs at peak performance without overloading any single system…

4. Device Drivers: Bridging Hardware and Software

Device drivers are specialized modules within the kernel that facilitate communication with hardware components — such as cameras, sensors, and displays. They translate high-level commands into device-specific operations, enabling seamless integration between the software and the physical hardware…

Analogy
Think of device drivers as the car’s onboard control modules and wiring systems. They act like the Electronic Control Units [ECUs] that interpret signals from the accelerator, brakes, and steering wheel to coordinate the car’s mechanical responses. In the same way, drivers translate the kernel’s commands into actions that hardware components can execute, ensuring every part of the car [or device] functions correctly…

5. File System Management: Organising Data Storage

The kernel’s File System Management is tasked with organising, storing, and retrieving data on the device. It maintains the structure of directories and files, manages permissions, and ensures data integrity, allowing for fast and secure access to stored information…

Analogy
Imagine that File System Management is the car’s dashboard and central computer that organises vital information. Just as the dashboard displays organised data [speed, fuel level, engine temperature, etc] and the car’s compartments keep everything neatly stored, the kernel organises files and data so that everything is accessible and well-maintained. It is like having a perfectly organised trunk where every tool and accessory has its designated place…

6. Inter-Process Communication & System Calls: The Communication Network

Inter-process communication [IPC] and System Calls form the channels through which user applications interact with the kernel. These mechanisms allow apps to request services like file access, process control, or hardware operations, ensuring that the kernel can serve as a reliable intermediary between user commands and system-level actions…

Analogy
Think of IPC and System Calls as the car’s internal wiring and sensor network. When you press the accelerator or turn the steering wheel, signals travel through a network of wires to the Engine Control Unit [ECU], which then adjusts performance accordingly. Similarly, the System Calls act as the direct communication lines between applications and the kernel, ensuring that every command is transmitted accurately — just like the reliable wiring that keeps your car running smoothly…

7. Security, Customisation, and Performance: Tuning The System

The kernel incorporates robust security measures — such as SELinux — to enforce permissions and protect the system from unauthorised access. Additionally, its modular design allows for customisation and performance tuning through loadable modules and configurable settings. This flexibility enables Android Engineers to optimise the system for specific hardware or application needs…

Analogy
Imagine this aspect as the car’s advanced safety systems and performance tuning options. The kernel’s security features are like airbags, anti-lock brakes, and alarm systems that protect you on the road. Customisation and performance tuning are similar to upgrading your car’s suspension, engine chip, or even selecting different driving modes [Eco, Sport, and Comfort] to tailor the driving experience. These features ensure that your “vehicle” runs safely, efficiently, and exactly to your preferences…

UP NEXT

The Hardware Abstraction Layer [HAL]: https://medium.com/@emmanuelmuturia/3dbb6e96cd43

CONCLUSION

Apart from the desire to drive your Car right now, I hope that you have now gotten a better and more technical understanding of The Linux Kernel in the context [pun intended] of The Android Architecture. Stay tuned for more content and until then, I hope that you remember what you learned today each time you go for your road trip :)…

REFERENCES

Platform Architecture. (2023, May 4). Android Developers. https://developer.android.com/guide/platform

Imagine that Process Management is your car’s transmission system. Just as a transmission shifts gears to optimise the engine’s power output based on driving conditions, the kernel’s scheduler dynamically allocates CPU time to various processes. This careful management prevents “traffic jams” in processing and ensures smooth, responsive performance — much like a well-tuned car seamlessly shifting through gears during a drive…

3. Memory Management: Allocating and Optimising Resources

Memory Management in the kernel involves dynamically allocating, tracking, and reclaiming Memory for processes. Techniques like Virtual Memory, Paging, and Caching ensure that each application has the necessary space to operate without interfering with others, maintaining system stability and efficiency…

Analogy
Consider that Memory Management is the car’s fuel injection and storage system. Just as fuel injectors precisely deliver the right amount of fuel to each engine cylinder and the car’s compartments are organised to store essentials, the kernel allocates the appropriate amount of memory to each process. This ensures that every part of the “engine” runs at peak performance without overloading any single system…

4. Device Drivers: Bridging Hardware and Software

Device drivers are specialized modules within the kernel that facilitate communication with hardware components — such as cameras, sensors, and displays. They translate high-level commands into device-specific operations, enabling seamless integration between the software and the physical hardware…

Analogy
Think of device drivers as the car’s onboard control modules and wiring systems. They act like the Electronic Control Units [ECUs] that interpret signals from the accelerator, brakes, and steering wheel to coordinate the car’s mechanical responses. In the same way, drivers translate the kernel’s commands into actions that hardware components can execute, ensuring every part of the car [or device] functions correctly…

5. File System Management: Organising Data Storage

The kernel’s File System Management is tasked with organising, storing, and retrieving data on the device. It maintains the structure of directories and files, manages permissions, and ensures data integrity, allowing for fast and secure access to stored information…

Analogy
Imagine that File System Management is the car’s dashboard and central computer that organises vital information. Just as the dashboard displays organised data [speed, fuel level, engine temperature, etc] and the car’s compartments keep everything neatly stored, the kernel organises files and data so that everything is accessible and well-maintained. It is like having a perfectly organised trunk where every tool and accessory has its designated place…

6. Inter-Process Communication & System Calls: The Communication Network

Inter-process communication [IPC] and System Calls form the channels through which user applications interact with the kernel. These mechanisms allow apps to request services like file access, process control, or hardware operations, ensuring that the kernel can serve as a reliable intermediary between user commands and system-level actions…

Analogy
Think of IPC and System Calls as the car’s internal wiring and sensor network. When you press the accelerator or turn the steering wheel, signals travel through a network of wires to the Engine Control Unit [ECU], which then adjusts performance accordingly. Similarly, the System Calls act as the direct communication lines between applications and the kernel, ensuring that every command is transmitted accurately — just like the reliable wiring that keeps your car running smoothly…

7. Security, Customisation, and Performance: Tuning The System

The kernel incorporates robust security measures — such as SELinux — to enforce permissions and protect the system from unauthorised access. Additionally, its modular design allows for customisation and performance tuning through loadable modules and configurable settings. This flexibility enables Android Engineers to optimise the system for specific hardware or application needs…

Analogy
Imagine this aspect as the car’s advanced safety systems and performance tuning options. The kernel’s security features are like airbags, anti-lock brakes, and alarm systems that protect you on the road. Customisation and performance tuning are similar to upgrading your car’s suspension, engine chip, or even selecting different driving modes [Eco, Sport, and Comfort] to tailor the driving experience. These features ensure that your “vehicle” runs safely, efficiently, and exactly to your preferences…

UP NEXT

The Hardware Abstraction Layer [HAL]: https://medium.com/@emmanuelmuturia/3dbb6e96cd43

CONCLUSION

Apart from the desire to drive your Car right now, I hope that you have now gotten a better and more technical understanding of The Linux Kernel in the context [pun intended] of The Android Architecture. Stay tuned for more content and until then, I hope that you remember what you learned today each time you go for your road trip :)…

REFERENCES

Platform Architecture. (2023, May 4). Android Developers. https://developer.android.com/guide/platform

items.title: How Not To Write UI Tests [feat. Test Tags & Semantics]
items.author: Emmanuel Muturia™
items.description:
I was trying to figure out why my Compose UI Tests were suddenly failing when I stumbled upon this article: https://tomasrepcik.dev/blog/2024/2024-02-13-test-tags-and-sematics/

BACKGROUND

At first, I wondered why Test Tags would not be the best for writing UI Tests in Jetpack Compose. However, reading that article provided me with insights that I had not considered throughout my journey in Testing [and Android in general]. In this article, we are going to learn why Test Tags might not be the best approach to writing and running our Compose UI Tests. If that sounds interesting, then please read on. This is: How Not To Write UI Tests [feat. Test Tags & Semantics]

WHAT ARE TEST TAGS?

If you are new to Compose UI Tests, then please check out this documentation to gain a decent foundation on the same and then return here to learn about Test Tags in Compose UI Tests: https://developer.android.com/develop/ui/compose/testing

Welcome back. As I was saying, Test Tags are a common way of conducting Compose UI Tests. Why is that? Well, that is because they are used to uniquely identify a node [Composable that is to be tested]. Think of them as actual name tags in real life that may be used to uniquely identify someone using their name. In the same way, Test Tags are used when we need to test a specific node when writing Compose UI Tests…

WHAT IS THEIR PROBLEM?

Well, it is not really about them having a problem but rather them having certain limitations that make them unsuitable for effectively writing and running Compose UI Tests. Without wasting any time, here are the Test Tag limitations that you should consider when writing Compose UI Tests:

1] Developer-Specific

  • Test Tags are intended only for testing purposes and are invisible to end-users or accessibility tools…
  • They need to be manually added by the developer, which can lead to errors if missed…

2] No Built-In Accessibility Support

  • Test Tags do not automatically enhance the accessibility of your app, unlike semantics (e.g., contentDescription), which are designed for screen readers and assistive technologies…

3] Not Always Attached Properly

  • Test Tags depend on developers explicitly adding them to Composables. If a developer forgets to set a test tag or assigns it incorrectly, the UI Tests may fail…

4] Ambiguity in Complex UIs

  • If multiple components share the same testTag, it can cause ambiguity in identifying which component the test should interact with, leading to Flaky Tests…

5] Limited to Testing Context

  • Test Tags are useful for testing but do not impact the User Experience [UX] directly. They do not improve the app’s usability or accessibility for real users…

CAN I STILL USE TEST TAGS, THOUGH?

Why not? While Test Tags have their fair share of limitations, here is where they may come in handy:

1] When Semantics Are Not Suitable for Testing Purposes

  • Test Tags are ideal when you need to create test-specific identifiers that are not necessarily related to the meaning or functionality of the UI element. This is especially useful when you do not want to affect the Accessibility layer of the app…
  • For instance, if you need to test an internal implementation detail or create a test-only identifier that does not need to be read by screen readers, a Test Tag is a good option. Here is an example:
Box(modifier = Modifier.testTag(tag = "TestTagForSomeElement")) {
    // Your UI content...
}

In this case, you are providing a tag that serves no accessibility purpose, but simply helps you locate the element in your tests…

2] When Identifying Unique or Complex UI Elements

  • Test Tags are useful when dealing with complex UI hierarchies or dynamic lists, where semantic identifiers like contentDescription might not be as clear or suitable…
  • If there are multiple similar items (e.g., list items) and the contentDescription would be too generic or difficult to differentiate, Test Tags can be a more straightforward way to uniquely identify them. Here is an example:
LazyColumn {
    items(items = listOfItems) { item ->
        Text(text = "Item ${item.id}", modifier = Modifier.testTag(tag = "Item-${item.id}"))
    }
}

3] When You Need a Stable Identifier for Testing

  • Test Tags are great when you need a stable identifier for UI elements, particularly in UI tests where the visual layout may change but the tag remains constant…
  • In some cases, contentDescription might be tied to the actual text or label of the UI element (which could change), whereas Test Tags provide a more stable reference that won’t be impacted by content changes. Here is an example:
Button(onClick = { /* Do something... */ }, modifier = Modifier.testTag(tag = "SubmitButton")) {
    Text(text = "Submit")
}

This ensures that your tests won’t break if the button text changes, as long as the Test Tag stays the same…

4] When You Need to Perform Tests on Non-Visible UI Elements

  • If you have UI elements that are not visible but are important for testing (such as invisible buttons, hidden fields, or invisible states), Test Tags can be an excellent solution…
  • These elements may not need semantic identifiers (like Content Descriptions) because they are not meant to be read by screen readers, but you still need a way to reference them in tests. Here is an example:
Box(modifier = Modifier.testTag(tag = "InvisibleElement").fillMaxSize().alpha(alpha = 0f)) {
    // UI content...
}

5] For Performance and Fine-Tuned Control

  • In some performance-sensitive tests, Test Tags can provide a lightweight and specific way to interact with UI elements, without the overhead of accessibility descriptions…
  • Test Tags provide a fine-tuned level of control over which elements to interact with during testing, especially when targeting elements for automation or when testing non-visible content…

WHAT ARE SEMANTICS?

Alright. We have talked about Test Tags and their limitations in Compose UI Testing. What then, are Semantics? Well, in Jetpack Compose, Semantics is a system used to describe the meaning and purpose of UI elements in an app. It is crucial for Accessibility, ensuring that users with disabilities can interact with and navigate the app using assistive technologies like screen readers. Semantics, particularly contentDescription, is a key part of this system…

  • Content Description: A description that can be added to a UI element (such as an image, button, or icon) to provide a text-based description of what the element does or represents. This text is then read aloud by screen readers for users with visual impairments, making the app more accessible…

In addition to contentDescription, semantics may also include other attributes that describe the element’s state, actions, or labels, such as stateDescription, role, text, and liveRegion…

For Example [Semantics]

Let us say we have a button that plays a video. In the UI, we add a contentDescription to the button for accessibility purposes:

Button(
    onClick = { /* Play video... */ },
    modifier = Modifier.semantics { contentDescription = "Play Video Button" }
) {
    Text(text = "Play")
}

Now, when we write UI tests, we can locate and interact with this button by referencing its contentDescription:

@Test
fun testPlayButtonExists() {
    onNodeWithContentDescription(label = "Play Video Button")
        .assertExists() // Verifies that the Play Video button exists...
}

This makes the test more robust, since the button’s purpose is clearly described, and the test will reliably work even if other parts of the UI change, as long as the contentDescription remains consistent…

WHY SHOULD I USE SEMANTICS IN MY COMPOSE UI TESTS?

Here is why you may be better off using Semantics [Content Description] in your Compose UI Tests:

1] User-Facing and Accessibility-Oriented

  • Content Descriptions (Semantics), such as contentDescription, are primarily designed to make the app more accessible to users with disabilities (e.g., screen readers)…
  • They improve accessibility for real users, not just during testing…

2] Stable and Meaningful

  • Unlike Test Tags, which may only serve the purpose of testing, semantics are naturally meaningful because they describe the function or content of the UI element…
  • This makes them inherently more stable for both accessibility and test purposes…

3] Automatically Attached

  • Many Composables automatically include useful semantics, such as contentDescription or text, making it easier to test UI elements without extra setup…
  • They’re tied directly to the component’s behaviour or purpose…

4] Improves Testing and Accessibility

  • Using semantics like contentDescription for testing not only improves test reliability but also ensures that the app is more accessible to all users, including those using assistive technologies…

5] Reduces Ambiguity in UI

  • Semantics typically describe the purpose or content of UI elements (e.g., a button to play a video), which can be more unique and descriptive than a test tag that may be used across multiple components…

THE CONCLUSION

By now, you know that Semantics offer a much better experience in Compose UI Testing just as they do in Accessibility. While Test Tags may not be the best solution when writing Compose UI Tests most of the time, they still have their place and you must be diligent enough to when that time is. Now, go forth and upgrade your app’s Testability [and Accessibility], will ya?

REFERENCES

Test your Compose layout. (2024). Android Developers. https://developer.android.com/develop/ui/compose/testing

‌Tomáš Repčík. (2024, February). Stop Using Test Tags in the Jetpack Compose Production Code. Tomáš Repčík — Blog. https://tomasrepcik.dev/blog/2024/2024-02-13-test-tags-and-sematics/
